exemplar.title: What counts as good evidence for a claim?

detailedExemplarData.title: What counts as good evidence for a claim?

strengths: Three specific, real-world objects are clearly identified with their unique contexts (the Premier League statistic, the womenâs football tweet, the Instagram story).
Each object is linked to the IA prompt âWhat counts as good evidence for a claim?â and the student offers a justification for inclusion.
The title explicitly states the IA task, demonstrating awareness of the exhibitionâs purpose.
weaknesses: Links between the objects and the prompt remain superficial and rely on sweeping assertions rather than critical analysis of evidence quality.
There is almost no engagement with counterarguments, source reliability or methodological limitations (e.g. no discussion of bias, sample size, or statistical validity).
The commentary is repetitive, formulaic (âa very good way to supportâ), and neglects TOK concepts such as Areas of Knowledge and Ways of Knowing.
Key evidential questions (reliability, validity, corroboration, author credibility, context) are either omitted or treated cursorily.
No synthesising conclusion ties the three objects together to address the prompt holistically.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The official examinerâs total of 9 is final. Although the commentary exhibits significant weaknesses in depth of analysis, critical evaluation and integration of TOK terminology, the work does meet almost all of the formal requirements for a high-level exhibition: three clearly defined objects, context descriptions, and consistent (if uncritical) justifications linked to the IA prompt. In reverse grading mode, to honour the examinerâs overall mark of 9, the single assessed criterion for the TOK Exhibition is awarded 9 out of 10. This allocation reflects the relative strength of fulfilling the structural and descriptive elements of the task, while acknowledging the substantive analytical deficiencies noted in the strand assessment.

annotations.comment: The title clearly identifies the IA task, but the student could enhance their framing by referencing TOK frameworks (Areas of Knowledge, Ways of Knowing) to situate their analysis within the discipline.

The student restates the IA prompt but fails to define criteria for âgood evidence.â They should explicate key TOK conceptsâsuch as reliability, validity, and biasâto clarify what makes evidence strong or weak.

The analysis omits discussion of how corroborating sources could reinforce or challenge this example. Evaluating multiple sources would demonstrate a more nuanced understanding of evidential strength.

The screenshot of a tweet is used as evidence without examining author credibility or potential irony. The student should assess how digital sources vary in reliability and the role of context in evaluating social media.

There is no mention of counterexamples or alternative interpretations. Engaging with potential counterclaims (e.g., other match outcomes) would demonstrate critical engagement and strengthen the evaluation of evidence.

The student introduces an Instagram Story but does not provide the actual image or screenshot. This omission weakens the evidence; they should include the visual source and assess its authenticity and temporal context.

Overgeneralisation is present when defining valid statistics. The student could improve by specifying how verification processes in quantitative methods contribute to reliability and under what conditions statistics might mislead.
The student overlooks audience perspectiveâdifferent audiences may interpret the same statistic differently. They should address how prior beliefs and contexts influence evidence acceptance.
The student describes the example but fails to evaluate its validity. They should consider whether a friendly match outcome is a robust test of the broader claim and discuss context and sample size.

The phrase âa very good way to supportâ is repeated, making the argument formulaic. They should replace these with precise evaluative language, drawing on criteria (e.g., validity, generalisability) to assess each form of evidence.

The commentary lacks mention of relevant Areas of Knowledge or Ways of Knowing. To deepen TOK analysis, the student should situate their objects within specific AOKs and discuss how different WOKs affect evidence evaluation.

The infographic is presented descriptively but lacks critical interrogation of its data collection and methodology. The student should question sample size, source reliability (Opta), and consider margins of error or selection bias.
The infographic lacks error bars or confidence intervals. The student could critique the absence of uncertainty measures, discussing how their inclusion strengthens statistical evidence.

This description is purely narrative, summarising public opinion without linking back to the TOK prompt. To strengthen, the student should analyze how statistics function as evidence in knowledge contexts, not merely recount controversy.

The student assumes correlation implies causation, claiming statistics âproveâ Ronaldo wasnât the problem. Recommend contrasting statistical evidence with alternative interpretations, discussing criteria that distinguish strong from weak evidence.
The student does not provide metadata such as timestamp or origin for the tweet. They should consider how temporality and sourcing details affect evidential strength and credibility.

The claim that âmen are superiorâ is a sweeping generalisation based on one match. The student should address representativeness and ethical implications before using an isolated incident to support a broad claim.
The term âverified evidenceâ is used loosely. They should clarify what verification entails in TOKâpeer review, reproducibilityâand distinguish this from mere acceptance of data.

The student presents the example but does not explicitly tie it back to the prompt âWhat counts as good evidence for a claim?â They should evaluate the evidential qualities of the example against this prompt.

The image of a birthday card is disconnected from the described Instagram story. The student should ensure coherence between chosen artifacts and their analysis, verifying that visual evidence matches the discussed context.

The inference that money alone explains personal reconciliation is simplistic. The student should consider alternative explanations and discuss how attribution fallacies undermine the strength of this social media evidence.

The narrative contains emotive language and moral judgment (e.g., âdeliberately destroyed his careerâ), which signals bias. The student should focus on evidence credibility and consider multiple possible motivations.

The exhibition ends without a synthesising conclusion. The student should add a final section that ties together the three objects, reflecting on how they collectively demonstrate âwhat counts as good evidence for a claim?â in a TOK context.

The conclusion overstates the strength of a single anecdotal social media post. The student should discuss corroboration and whether this example generalises sufficiently to support the claim âmoney is power.â

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content: The exhibition does not reach the standard described by the other levels or does not use one of the IA prompts provided.

The exhibition presents
three objects, but the
real-world contexts of
these objects are not
stated, or the images
presented may be
highly generic images of
types of object rather
than of specific
real-world objects. Links
between the objects
and the selected IA
prompt are made, but
these are minimal,
tenuous, or it is not
clear what the student is
trying to convey.
There is very little
justification offered for
the inclusion of each
object in the exhibition.
The commentary on the
objects is highly
descriptive or consists
only of unsupported
assertions.

The exhibition identifies three objects, although the real-world contexts of the objects may be implied rather than explicitly stated. Basic links between the objects and the selected IA prompt are made, but the explanation of these links is unconvincing and/or unfocused. There is a superficial justification for the inclusion of each object in the exhibition. Reasons for the inclusion of the objects are offered, but these are not supported by appropriate evidence and/or lack relevance to the selected IA prompt. There may be significant repetition across the justifications of the different objects

The exhibition identifies three objects, although the real-world contexts of these objects may be vaguely or imprecisely stated. There is some explanation of the links between the three objects and the selected IA prompt. There is some justification for the inclusion of each object in the exhibition. Some of the points are supported by evidence and references to the selected IA prompt.

The exhibition identifies three objects and their real-world contexts. Links between each of the three objects and the selected IA prompt are explained, although this explanation may lack precision and clarity in parts. There is a justification of the contribution that each individual object makes to the exhibition. Many of the points are supported by appropriate evidence and references to the selected IA prompt.

The exhibition clearly identifies three objects and their specific real-world contexts. Links between each of the three objects and the selected IA prompt are clearly made and well-explained. There is a strong justification of the particular contribution that each individual object makes to the exhibition. All, or nearly all, of the points are well-supported by appropriate evidence and explicit references to the selected IA prompt.

rubricCriterionStrands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
